icd_category: J67.9 — Hypersensitivity pneumonitis due to unspecified organic dust
one_liner: A 52-year-old dairy farmer presents with 4 months of recurrent episodes of cough, dyspnea, fever, and malaise that start 4-8 hours after baling moldy hay and resolve when he is away from the barn.

stage2.hpi_extension: Each episode begins with flu-like symptoms (fevers, chills, myalgias), a dry cough, and shortness of breath within 4-8 hours of entering his hay loft. Symptoms resolve over 1-2 days if he avoids the barn, and his wife has noticed his cough and breathing are markedly better when they travel out of town. In the past month, he has also developed more persistent dyspnea on exertion and a 7-pound weight loss. He denies sick contacts, hemoptysis, or chest pain.
stage2.social_history: Lifelong dairy farmer, handles moldy hay daily. Never-smoker. No pet birds. No recent travel.
stage2.physical_exam: General: mildly diaphoretic, appears fatigued. Lungs: bibasilar inspiratory crackles (fine Velcro rales), no wheezes, no rhonchi. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2. Extremities: no clubbing, no edema, no cyanosis. Skin: no rashes. HEENT: clear oropharynx, no lymphadenopathy.

lab_results.result: High-resolution CT chest: diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities with scattered centrilobular nodules and mosaic attenuation with air trapping on expiratory imaging, most prominent in the upper and mid lung zones. No honeycombing, no pleural effusion, no mass.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodules, and mosaic air trapping in the upper lobes of a farmer with recurrent post-exposure symptoms are classic for subacute hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

lab_results.result: Serum IgG precipitins positive against Saccharopolyspora rectivirgula (thermophilic actinomycetes, farmer's lung antigen). ANA negative, RF negative, ANCA negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Positive precipitins confirm prior exposure and sensitization to the offending antigen and support the diagnosis of farmer's lung in the right clinical context.

lab_results.result: Pulmonary function tests: restrictive pattern with FEV1/FVC 0.82, FVC 62% predicted, TLC 68% predicted, and decreased DLCO (54% predicted).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Restrictive physiology with reduced diffusion capacity is characteristic of interstitial lung disease from hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

stage5.final_clue: Bronchoalveolar lavage shows a marked lymphocytosis with a low CD4/CD8 ratio. Counseled to avoid the barn and started on a tapering course of oral prednisone, he has complete resolution of symptoms within 2 weeks.

debrief.classic_presentation: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (also called extrinsic allergic alveolitis) is a non-IgE-mediated inflammatory lung disease triggered by inhaled organic antigens. Classic exposures include moldy hay (farmer's lung, due to thermophilic actinomycetes), bird droppings and feathers (bird fancier's lung), and mold in hot tubs or humidifiers. Acute/subacute forms present with recurrent flu-like symptoms, cough, and dyspnea 4-8 hours after exposure that improve with avoidance. The chronic form can mimic idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
debrief.pathophysiology: HP is a mixed type III (immune-complex) and type IV (T-cell mediated) hypersensitivity reaction to inhaled organic antigens. In sensitized individuals, repeated antigen exposure leads to lymphocytic alveolitis, poorly formed non-caseating granulomas, and eventually fibrosis if exposure continues. Bronchoalveolar lavage shows a marked CD8-predominant lymphocytosis (low CD4/CD8 ratio), distinguishing HP from sarcoidosis (high CD4/CD8).
debrief.treatment_overview: The cornerstone of treatment is strict avoidance of the offending antigen. Acute and subacute episodes respond well to systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisone), which accelerate recovery. Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis with established fibrosis may progress despite avoidance and steroids; antifibrotic agents (pirfenidone, nintedanib) and lung transplantation may be considered in advanced disease.
high_yield_pearls: Farmer's lung is caused by thermophilic actinomycetes (Saccharopolyspora) in moldy hay.
HP is a mixed type III and type IV hypersensitivity reaction; onset is 4-8 hours after exposure.
HRCT shows upper lobe-predominant ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodules, and air trapping.
BAL lymphocytosis with a low CD4/CD8 ratio distinguishes HP from sarcoidosis (high CD4/CD8).
Chronic HP can cause irreversible fibrosis that mimics idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Respiratory chapter, 'Restrictive lung diseases — Hypersensitivity pneumonitis' section.
